frozen_components: VAE decoder (frozen after pretraining during CALM training and inference)
architecture.summary: CALM reframes language modeling from discrete next-token prediction to continuous next-vector prediction. A context-free variational autoencoder (VAE) compresses K tokens into a single continuous latent vector with >99.9% reconstruction accuracy. A standard Transformer backbone processes the discrete token stream and outputs a hidden state, which an energy-based generative head (stacked residual MLPs) uses to predict the next latent vector via a strictly proper scoring rule (energy score). The predicted vector is decoded back to discrete tokens via the frozen VAE decoder, completing the autoregressive loop. Information flows: discrete tokens → VAE encoder → latent vector → Transformer → energy head → predicted latent vector → VAE decoder → reconstructed tokens.

claims.statement: a latent vector of just 32 dimensions is sufficient to achieve high-fidelity reconstruction, with a token-level accuracy of over 99.9% when grouping K=4 tokens
CALM significantly improves the performance-compute trade-off, achieving the performance of strong discrete baselines at a significantly lower computational cost
BrierLM exhibits a Pearson correlation coefficient of -0.966 and a Spearman's rank correlation of -0.991 with cross-entropy loss
reduces the number of generative steps by a factor of K
the autoencoder is exceptionally lightweight; with a shallow architecture and a modest hidden dimension of 512, its computational overhead is nearly negligible compared to that of language model
Algorithm 1 (likelihood-free temperature sampling via rejection) generates samples distributed exactly according to the target temperature distribution for any arbitrary temperature τ
BrierLM is strictly proper, theoretically ensuring a fair comparison of model capabilities

a naive metric like the raw likelihood of the observed outcome fails the principle of being uniquely optimized when the model recovers the data distribution, instead incentivizing overconfident predictions
